title: Drop scones
subtitle: Scottish dropped scones, cooked on a griddle and eaten warm with butter
excerpt: Twenty Scottish drop scones (also called Scotch pancakes): a thick batter of self-raising flour, egg, milk, and a little sugar, dropped from a spoon onto a hot buttered griddle and cooked until bubbles appear across the surface, then flipped. Ready in 20 minutes.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the dropped scone chapter documents a thick flour batter dropped from a spoon onto a greased griddle, cooked on both sides until risen and golden. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton's griddle cake section documents the same method for dropped scones, noting that the batter should be thick enough to hold a mound shape on the griddle before the heat begins to spread it. Dropped scones (Scotch pancakes) are documented continuously in Scottish domestic cookery from the Victorian period; the recipe here follows the public-domain method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, then freeze in a bag layered with baking paper. Reheat in a dry pan or toaster.
recipe.batchNotes: The batter keeps for 2 hours at room temperature — cook in batches as you need them.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the batter and rest it for 10 minutes before cooking — the resting time lets the flour hydrate fully and produces a more even rise.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: griddle or heavy frying pan, medium heat — not oven baked

recipeTools.notes: Or a heavy, flat-based frying pan. Non-stick makes the job easier.
For consistent drop size — or use a dessertspoon.
A thin, flexible palette knife or fish slice for flipping.

glossaryTerms.definition: Testing that a griddle or heavy pan is at the right temperature before dropping scone batter onto it. Drop a teaspoon of batter onto the surface: it should sizzle immediately, begin to set at the edges within 30 seconds, and develop bubbles across the surface within 90 seconds. Too cool: the batter spreads flat and takes too long to set, producing pale, thin scones. Too hot: the edges burn before the centre bubbles.

content.text: Drop scones require no oven: the batter goes directly onto a hot griddle. The flip timing is the only skill. Look for bubbles across the entire surface and a matte (not glossy) appearance at the edges — both signs the batter has set enough to flip cleanly. Use the
before committing a full spoonful of batter.

items.prepNote: enough to make a thick, dropping consistency
melted — for the batter and a little for the griddle

content.text: Whisk together the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{baking-powder}} in a bowl. Make a well in the centre. Add the {{eggs}} eggs and half the {{whole-milk}} milk and whisk from the centre outward until a smooth, thick batter forms. Whisk in the melted {{unsalted-butter}}. Add the remaining milk gradually until the batter falls from a spoon in a thick, slow ribbon — it should be thicker than pancake batter and mound slightly on the spoon. Rest for 10 minutes.

Heat a griddle or heavy frying pan over medium heat. Brush with a very little butter. Test the heat with a small drop of batter — it should sizzle and set at the edges within 30 seconds. Drop dessertspoons of batter onto the griddle, spacing them 3 cm apart. Cook for 1.5–2 minutes until bubbles have formed across the entire surface and the edges look matte. Flip once with a spatula and cook for a further 1 minute until the undersides are golden. Keep warm wrapped in a clean cloth while you cook the remaining batches.
Serve warm with salted butter and jam, golden syrup, or a thin spread of clotted cream. Drop scones are best eaten within 30 minutes of cooking.
